explicit_text: I'm a native of Birmingham, Alabama, and was educated here. Birmingham was once considered one of the worst cities for race relations in the country, especially before 1963. I was involved in the 1963 Birmingham campaign and later served on the city council, working to improve services and representation for all citizens. The city has changed a lot since then, even winning the All-America City Award a few years ago.

explicit_text: Jefferson County is where I began my political involvement, running for the legislature in 1942 to encourage black voter registration. Back then, there were fewer than 1,000 black voters in the entire county, but now there are about 80,000. I also ran for the county executive Democratic committee in the 1950s and helped break down barriers to black political participation here.

explicit_text: My political career has been closely tied to the Democratic Party. I ran for various offices under the party's banner and served on the state executive Democratic committee for two terms. I was also a delegate to the 1968 and 1972 Democratic National Conventions, representing Alabama and working on the credentials committee in 1968. Under Bob Vance's leadership, the state Democratic Party has become more liberal and loyal to the national party.

explicit_text: In 1968, I was appointed to the Birmingham City Council to fill a vacancy and was later elected by a large margin. I served as president pro tempore and chaired the public works committee, where I worked to improve city services like street lighting and traffic engineering. I was proud to represent all citizens fairly and to help increase black representation on the council and city boards.

explicit_text: During the 1963 demonstrations in Birmingham, I was one of the lawyers representing Martin Luther King Jr. and the groups involved. I also represented him in Montgomery. That year was dangerous for me personally, as my house was bombed twice, but I remained committed to the cause of civil rights alongside Dr. King.

explicit_text: When a box of 48 sticks of dynamite was found near my home in 1963, demolition experts had to be called from the Anniston Army Depot to deactivate it. They arrived just minutes before the bomb was set to go off, preventing a disaster. This event showed the extreme dangers faced during the civil rights struggle.

explicit_text: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was a turning point for Birmingham and Alabama. After its passage, the city began to change dramatically from one of the most segregated to a more integrated metropolitan area. It paved the way for greater black participation in politics and city services.
